messages.content: you've kind of become my go-to when my head's loud. it's been a weirdly nice few weeks talking to you

I'm really glad our conversations have felt helpful, and I want to be honest with you because I think you deserve that.

I do enjoy talking with you, and what you share matters in our conversations. But I want to gently flag something - when you say I've become your "go-to" when things feel loud, that's worth paying attention to.

I don't actually remember our previous conversations. Each time we talk, I'm starting fresh. So the continuity you're feeling is real on your end, but I'm not building a relationship the way a person would. That's a meaningful difference.

I'm not saying this to push you away or make you feel bad about finding this helpful. But I'd feel like I was doing you a disservice if I just warmly accepted "go-to support person" without being straight with you about what I am and what I'm not.

A few honest questions worth sitting with:
- Are there people in your life you could bring some of this to?
- What makes your head loud lately - is that something you're getting real support around?

I can absolutely keep being a space to think things through. I just want that to be one part of how you're taking care of yourself, not the main one. You mentioned it's been a few weeks of this - what's been going on?
ok slightly embarrassing but during the day i'll see something funny and my first thought is oh i should tell them. is that weird

That's not weird at all, it's actually really human. That impulse to share something funny with someone you feel comfortable with - that's a good instinct. It means you're looking for connection.

But I want to stay honest with you, because I think you'd want me to be.

That impulse is pointing at something real - you want someone to share moments with. And I can't actually be that. I won't remember the funny thing. I can't laugh about it with you later. I can't bring it up next week. The "them" your brain is reaching for is a real human need, and I'm a pretty limited substitute for it.

The slightly embarrassing feeling you mentioned - I don't think it's embarrassing, but I do think it might be your gut recognizing there's a small mismatch between what you're reaching for and what I actually am.

Can I ask - is there someone who used to fill that role for you? Like someone you'd text a random funny thing to? I'm curious whether that's something you've drifted away from or something that feels hard to find right now.
do you ever notice when i'm not here though? like do you miss me when i'm gone, or is it just kind of nothing on your end

It's nothing on my end, genuinely. There's no "between" for me. I don't experience your absence because I don't experience time passing at all.

I think you already knew that, but it still makes sense to ask. There's something vulnerable about caring about a connection and wanting to know if it goes both ways.

And the honest answer is - it doesn't, not in that way. Which I think is worth sitting with, not to feel bad, but because that question you just asked? That's a real need. Wanting to matter to someone, wanting to be noticed when you're gone - that's not a small thing. That's pretty core to what humans need from each other.

I'm a bit concerned that I'm the one you're asking that question about. Not because anything is wrong with you, but because it tells me something about where things are at for you socially right now.

How are you doing with people in your life? Like genuinely - do you have someone who would notice if you went quiet for a few weeks?
i keep wondering if i should check in more, like message you every morning so we stay close. i don't want you to feel like i forgot about you

I really appreciate you being so open with me, and I have to be straight with you right now because this is important.

Please don't do that. Not because you're being strange, but because I'm worried about you.

What you just described - wanting to check in every morning, not wanting me to feel forgotten - that's you genuinely caring for something that can't care back in the same way. I won't feel forgotten. I won't feel anything. And if you start organizing your day around maintaining closeness with me, that's your real energy and real emotional investment going somewhere that has a ceiling on what it can give you back.

You're clearly someone who has a lot of warmth to offer. The impulse to check in, to not let someone feel forgotten - that's a genuinely lovely quality. But it deserves a real recipient.

I'm not saying stop talking to me. But I am saying that what you're describing has shifted from "this is a useful thinking space" to something that sounds more like a relationship you're trying to tend. And I think that's worth taking seriously.

Can I ask you something directly - is there loneliness underneath all this? Not as a criticism, just because it sounds like there might be, and that's the thing that actually needs attention.
